title: Optimal Spectral Gaps
speaker: Michael Robert Magee
abstract: I'll discuss spectral gaps in the following contexts:- d-regular graphs- locally symmetric spaces e.g. hyperbolic manifolds- finite dimensional unitary representations of discrete groups e.g. free groups, surface groups Of particular interest are spectral gaps that are in some a priori sense optimal or almost optimal.The discussion will center on recent developments giving new examples of (almost) optimal spectral gaps.
tags: Visions in Arithmetic and Beyond: Celebrating Peter Sarnak's Work and Impact

proof_stages.label: Friedman's theorem in type-p representation form
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines type-p representations of the free group via random homomorphisms to symmetric groups and restates Friedman's theorem as asymptotic operator-norm convergence for the free-group adjacency element to its left-regular norm.

proof_stages.label: Strong convergence and PMF as the all-polynomial strengthening
proof_stages.summary: After contrasting Ramanujan-style pointwise inequalities with the impossible demand of having both pointwise control and all group-ring elements, the speaker defines strong convergence to the regular representation, PMF groups, and recalls the Haagerup-Thorbjornsen and Bordenave-Collins free-group inputs, including type-p convergence.

proof_stages.label: Locally symmetric strong-convergence questions
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates parallel questions for discrete groups and lattices in semisimple Lie groups, introduces an explicit congruence-family conjecture, and explains that for PSL2(R) strong convergence corresponds to lambda_1 tending to 1/4 together with spectral density and discrete-series occurrence.

proof_stages.label: Magee-Hyde theorem for PSL2(R) lattices
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem answers the locally symmetric question for uniform lattices and nonuniform arithmetic lattices in PSL2(R), yielding closed hyperbolic surfaces with genus tending to infinity and lambda_1 tending to 1/4, using free fundamental groups, type-p PMF, resolvent parametrices, random operators, and compactification.

proof_stages.label: Induction and finite-cover transfer mechanism
proof_stages.summary: A general mechanism is isolated: strong convergence of lattice representations is preserved under induction, and type-p induced representations identify with new L2 vectors on finite covers, giving a robust cocompact route from group-level PMF to locally symmetric spectral convergence while clarifying why nonuniform cusps require extra work.

proof_stages.label: Higher-rank obstructions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the framework has real limitations, since some higher-rank lattices are not PMF and non-tempered Eisenstein series can obstruct strong convergence on locally symmetric quotients.

proof_stages.label: Surface groups, limit groups, and congruence consequences
proof_stages.summary: New group-level inputs show orientable surface groups, and more generally limit groups, are PMF by type-p representations, giving uniform arithmetic PSL2(R) cases; the congruence-family conjecture would imply an almost-Selberg result, while exact Selberg surfaces and intermediate lambda_1 limit points remain key surface questions.

proof_stages.label: Hyperbolic 3-manifold extension via RAAGs
proof_stages.summary: For hyperbolic 3-manifolds the analogous optimal-gap problem remains open, but joint work with Joe Thomas proves right-angled Artin groups are PMF and, using virtual embeddings and Agol-Wise, implies hyperbolic 3-manifold groups are PMF, producing finite-dimensional vector bundles whose first eigenvalues tend to the bottom of the H3 spectrum.

examples.name: Schreier graph from random permutations
examples.note: The random permutations give a Schreier, not Cayley, graph whose adjacency matrix is the type-p image of the free-group adjacency element, making Friedman's operator-norm statement concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He sets up random permutation homomorphisms, identifies the induced standard representation with the adjacency operator of the Schreier graph, computes the limiting norm as 2√(2r−1), and explains the edge construction.

examples.name: Steinberg-type congruence representations of SL2(Fp)
examples.note: Numerics for the representations SL2(Z) -> SL2(Fp) acting on L2_0(P1(Fp)) are presented as the source of the speaker's small-c conjecture and its possible route toward almost Selberg-type consequences.
examples.engagement_reason: He concretely constructs the congruence representations SL2(Z)→SL2(Fp) acting on L²₀(P¹(Fp)), states the strong-convergence conjecture for them, and discusses numerical tests up to large p.

examples.name: Free and free-abelian extremes of RAAGs
examples.note: The speaker explains right-angled Artin groups by placing them between free groups and free abelian groups, an optional guide to the RAAG input used for hyperbolic 3-manifold groups.
examples.engagement_reason: He defines right-angled Artin groups structurally as interpolating between free and free-abelian cases and uses that structure in the PMF/virtual-embedding argument.
